entities.name: darśana
entities.cite: Just as, by its earlier perceivings — from afar, or in deep gloom — sight

entities.name: viṣaya
entities.cite: makes the object other than it is and determines it quite otherwise

axioms.name: adhyavasyati
axioms.cite: determining it with full assertoric confidence

denials.name: avasthapayati
denials.cite: sight does not withhold judgement until conditions improve

claims.name: anyathakrtya
claims.cite: sight makes the object other than it is

rejected_readings.label: epistemic_caution
rejected_readings.rendering: Sight, from a distance or in deep gloom, provisionally determines the object while reserving final judgment until closer inspection.
rejected_readings.why: The rendering suggests epistemic caution — withholding confident judgment until conditions improve. But the commentary and axioms show the opposite: darsana delivers determinate, confident verdicts at each stage (adhyavasyati), which are wrong but asserted with full confidence. This contradicts the denial that darsana does NOT withhold judgment.

rejected_readings.label: object_multiplicity
rejected_readings.rendering: Sight perceives different objects when viewing from a distance versus close up, with the distant view showing one object and the close view revealing another.
rejected_readings.why: Treats the staged misapprehension as evidence of multiple objects, when the commentary emphasizes there is one object throughout: 'The endpoint is veridical, and the errors were its road.' The axioms show darsana making a single viṣaya appear otherwise at each stage, not perceiving different objects. Multiple distinct objects would not illustrate the normal path to truth through committed error.

notes: Verse 1.89 is the protasis for 1.90, establishing perception (darsana) as the model for staged apprehension. The core commitment: darsana makes the object appear otherwise (anyathakrtya) and asserts it with full confidence (adhyavasyati) at each stage, yet reaches truth through this committed error. The six-sort system fits naturally: cognition (perception) and manifestation (the object as it appears) are the primary sorts.
